behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real filesystem watch behavior (recursive/child-path event delivery), which can differ by OS/watch backend. A mocked watcher can always emit the child-path event and miss real failures where subdirectory writes are never observed. No AI dependency, no multi-agent chain.
behaviors.test_description: Run the real daemon against a real temp directory tree. Create `root/sub/a.txt` after watcher startup and assert exactly one transformed output is written for that file without restart.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: Behavior text is about dedup logic itself (path + time-window decision), which is local deterministic state/algorithm. Rejected Integration/System: no required OS/network/subprocess evidence in this behavior; module wiring is not the failure boundary stated.
behaviors.test_description: Test the dedup component directly with a controllable clock: first request for path is accepted, second request within window is rejected, and a request after window expiry is accepted.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly depends on OS-emitted duplicate filesystem events. Mocking event emission can hide real edge cases in event timing/type patterns from real watcher backends. No AI dependency.
behaviors.test_description: With real watcher active, perform one file write pattern that is known to trigger multiple OS events (e.g., write+rename or chunked write) and assert pipeline/output side effects occur exactly once for that path.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: Requires real OS filesystem lifecycle semantics (delete/recreate inode, watcher invalidation/rebind). In-process fakes usually do not reproduce this failure mode accurately. No AI dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon watching a real temp dir, remove it, recreate it at same path, then create a new input file and verify it is processed without restarting daemon.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Primary failure boundary is module handoff/order logic (ingest queue + timestamp extraction + scheduler), testable in-process with local filesystem metadata or injected timestamp provider. Rejected System: behavior text does not require validating OS event-delivery quirks, only ordering rule by creation timestamp. No AI dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Wire watcher intake->queue->processor in one process, submit multiple file arrivals with controlled creation timestamps (or controlled timestamp provider), and assert processing order matches ascending creation time.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: Depends on real filesystem write-in-progress behavior and watcher timing. Mocked/stubbed file writes often hide partial-write races and readiness heuristics (size stabilization, lock behavior). No AI dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Using real filesystem and watcher, start a slow/chunked write to a new file, trigger arrival event naturally, assert daemon does not process mid-write, then assert processing occurs after final flush/close.
